question: Explain the concept of 'technical debt' in the context of developing AI systems and describe two software engineering practices that can help mitigate it.
expectedPoints: Definition of technical debt as the implied cost of rework caused by choosing an easy solution now instead of a better approach that would take longer.
Practice 1: Rigorous version control (e.g., Git) for data, model weights, and code to track changes and facilitate collaboration.
Practice 2: Comprehensive and automated testing, including unit tests for components and integration tests for the entire AI pipeline to ensure reliability.

question: Why is continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) particularly important for building reliable, human-centred AI systems? Provide one specific example.
expectedPoints: CI/CD allows for the frequent and automated testing of new model versions and code changes, ensuring they integrate smoothly and do not break existing functionality.
This is crucial for human-centred AI as it provides a safety net for changes, allowing for rapid iteration while maintaining system stability and user trust.
Example: Automatically running a battery of fairness tests on a new model version in a staging environment before it is deployed to production, preventing a biased model from reaching users.

question: Identify one key difference between testing traditional software and testing a machine learning model.
expectedPoints: Traditional software is tested for deterministic correctness (same input always produces the same expected output), while ML models are tested for statistical performance and behavior (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall) on a representative dataset, as the output is probabilistic.
